visa_types.name: Invitation Visa
visa_types.documents_required:
Submitting all required documents does not guarantee a visa. 
Each applicant needs to complete and sign his own form and apply individually.
Checklist for Invitation Visa
- Visa application form, filled in completely in block letters, and signed by the applicant.
- For minors, the form is submitted and signed by the person exercising parental responsibility or a legal guardian. If a parent/legal guardian is not travelling with the minor, their travel consent must be provided as required by the laws of the minor’s country of residence.
- Valid Passport (valid for at least 3 months longer than intended stay), +1 photocopy of the bio-data page of the said passport 
- Previous passports, (if any) +1 photocopy of pages bearing particulars of the applicant
- 2 recent passport size photographs (not more than 3 months old) 
- Visa Fee + VFS Charges (if not already paid online)
- Invitation letter from a recognized public or private body, institution, or organization. The letter must state the purpose of the event, duration of stay, reason for invitation, and, if applicable, confirm responsibility for travel and accommodation costs. 
- Return flight ticket or booking. 
- Proof of accommodation, such as a hotel reservation, hospitality declaration, or a letter from the inviting entity confirming accommodation arrangements. 
- Travel medical insurance valid in all Schengen countries, with minimum coverage of EUR 30,000 for medical expenses and repatriation 
- Proof of sufficient financial means for the stay, when costs are not covered by the inviting entity. Applicants must provide bank statements and pay slips for the last 6 months. If multiple bank accounts are held, the account where the salary is paid must also be included.
A detailed travel itinerary must be provided to show that Italy is the main or sole destination. Your flight booking should include an Italian airport, or be supported by proof of onward travel to Italy. Hotel reservations must be valid and confirmed.

visa_types.processing_time:
Regarding the processing times for visa applications, it is important to distinguish between Schengen visas (type C) and national visas (type D).
For Schengen visas (type C), the processing time is 15 calendar days from the date the application is deemed admissible, extendable in exceptional cases up to a maximum of 45 days if a more thorough examination of the application is required.
For national visas (type D), the general processing time is 90 days from the date of application.
Exceptions include family reunification visas and subordinate work visas, which must be decided within a maximum of 30 days, and self-employed work visas, for which the decision must be made within 120 days.
The Embassy will make efforts to expedite the decision on the application before departure date.

visa_types.overview:
Overview
The Uniform Schengen Visa (USV) – Type C allows applicants to travel for short stays of up to 90 days in any 180-day period.
The invitation visa allows foreign nationals to enter Italy for a short stay upon invitation from public or private bodies, institutions, or organizations of proven standing, in order to participate in political, scientific, cultural, or institutional events or activities. This visa category is intended for official or qualified guests whose presence is linked to specific, formally recognized initiatives.
If the purpose of the stay is to carry out economic or commercial activities, a business visa must be requested instead.
Article 17 of the Consolidated Immigration Act (Legislative Decree 286/1998) also allows the Chief of Police (Questore) to authorize entry for a foreign national who must participate in judicial proceedings concerning them, in order to exercise their right of defence. Legal consultants or lawyers appointed by the applicant may be issued a business visa, provided all legal requirements are met.
Before submission, ensure your competency to submit your application.
Territorial competence
You must be a citizen and/or legal resident of Burkina Faso, Côte d’Ivoire, Liberia, Niger or Sierra Leone, or be legally present there. If you are only present temporarily in these Countries, then you must justify why you cannot apply where you habitually reside. 
Material competence
If the Embassy of Italy in Ivory Coast is not competent, your application and documents will be returned, visa fees refunded, and a written decision of incompetence issued. Please verify the competent Italian Embassy/Consulate before booking.
visa_types.full_text:
## Overview
The Uniform Schengen Visa (USV) – Type C allows applicants to travel for short stays of up to 90 days in any 180-day period.
The invitation visa allows foreign nationals to enter Italy for a short stay upon invitation from public or private bodies, institutions, or organizations of proven standing, in order to participate in political, scientific, cultural, or institutional events or activities. This visa category is intended for official or qualified guests whose presence is linked to specific, formally recognized initiatives.
If the purpose of the stay is to carry out economic or commercial activities, a business visa must be requested instead.
Article 17 of the Consolidated Immigration Act (Legislative Decree 286/1998) also allows the Chief of Police (Questore) to authorize entry for a foreign national who must participate in judicial proceedings concerning them, in order to exercise their right of defence. Legal consultants or lawyers appointed by the applicant may be issued a business visa, provided all legal requirements are met.
Before submission, ensure your competency to submit your application.
Territorial competence
You must be a citizen and/or legal resident of Burkina Faso, Côte d’Ivoire, Liberia, Niger or Sierra Leone, or be legally present there. If you are only present temporarily in these Countries, then you must justify why you cannot apply where you habitually reside. 
Material competence
If the Embassy of Italy in Ivory Coast is not competent, your application and documents will be returned, visa fees refunded, and a written decision of incompetence issued. Please verify the competent Italian Embassy/Consulate before booking.

## Visa Fees

[ita > civ > en > Table:Tourist > Visa Fees 1]

Declaration of Value for Study Titles 
2. Service Charges 

[ita > civ > en > Table:Tourist > Visa Fees 2]

[ita > civ > en > Table:Tourist > Visa Fees 3]
(The fee change is as per the currency exchange rates)
The above-mentioned service charges include VAT 18%. Fee amount can be paid only in local currency.
The fee will be applicable per set of documents to be legalized (each set will contain a maximum of 3 documents, (i.e) originals and translations).
Visa fees are established by the applicable legislation and are periodically updated.
No other fees apply beyond those listed on this website.

## Documents Required
Submitting all required documents does not guarantee a visa. 
Each applicant needs to complete and sign his own form and apply individually.
Checklist for Invitation Visa
- Visa application form, filled in completely in block letters, and signed by the applicant.
- For minors, the form is submitted and signed by the person exercising parental responsibility or a legal guardian. If a parent/legal guardian is not travelling with the minor, their travel consent must be provided as required by the laws of the minor’s country of residence.
- Valid Passport (valid for at least 3 months longer than intended stay), +1 photocopy of the bio-data page of the said passport 
- Previous passports, (if any) +1 photocopy of pages bearing particulars of the applicant
- 2 recent passport size photographs (not more than 3 months old) 
- Visa Fee + VFS Charges (if not already paid online)
- Invitation letter from a recognized public or private body, institution, or organization. The letter must state the purpose of the event, duration of stay, reason for invitation, and, if applicable, confirm responsibility for travel and accommodation costs. 
- Return flight ticket or booking. 
- Proof of accommodation, such as a hotel reservation, hospitality declaration, or a letter from the inviting entity confirming accommodation arrangements. 
- Travel medical insurance valid in all Schengen countries, with minimum coverage of EUR 30,000 for medical expenses and repatriation 
- Proof of sufficient financial means for the stay, when costs are not covered by the inviting entity. Applicants must provide bank statements and pay slips for the last 6 months. If multiple bank accounts are held, the account where the salary is paid must also be included.
A detailed travel itinerary must be provided to show that Italy is the main or sole destination. Your flight booking should include an Italian airport, or be supported by proof of onward travel to Italy. Hotel reservations must be valid and confirmed.

## Photo Specifications
Photographs 
Please provide your recent photograph (not more than 6 months old). The photographs should be in color: - 
- Taken against a clear background so that features are distinguishable and contrast against the background;
- Clear and of good quality;
- printed on normal photographic paper;
- Unmounted - 45 mm x 35 mm in size;
- Full face (without sunglasses and normally without a hat or other head covering, unless the applicant wears such items because of their religious belief or ethnic background).

## Processing Time
Regarding the processing times for visa applications, it is important to distinguish between Schengen visas (type C) and national visas (type D).
For Schengen visas (type C), the processing time is 15 calendar days from the date the application is deemed admissible, extendable in exceptional cases up to a maximum of 45 days if a more thorough examination of the application is required.
For national visas (type D), the general processing time is 90 days from the date of application.
Exceptions include family reunification visas and subordinate work visas, which must be decided within a maximum of 30 days, and self-employed work visas, for which the decision must be made within 120 days.
The Embassy will make efforts to expedite the decision on the application before departure date.

## Download Forms
Please find the Visa/Legalization application form below. Application form must be submitted completed and signed by the applicant. Visa application forms can be provided at the Center. 
Visa application forms are free of charge. If you have any problems downloading a form, please contact our Visa and Legalization Application Centre.

visa_types.name: Work - Self-Employment Visa Long Stay
visa_types.documents_required:
Submitting all required documents does not guarantee a visa. 
Each applicant needs to complete and sign his own form and apply individually.
- General documents requiredVisa application form, filled in completely in block letters, and signed by the applicant.
- For minors, the form is submitted and signed by the person exercising parental responsibility or a legal guardian. If a parent/legal guardian is not travelling with the minor, their travel consent must be provided as required by the laws of the minor’s country of residence.
- Valid Passport (valid for at least 3 months longer than intended stay), +1 photocopy of the bio-data page of the said passport 
- Previous passports, (if any) +1 photocopy of pages bearing particulars of the applicant
- 2 recent passport size photographs (not more than 3 months old) 
- Visa Fee + VFS Charges (if not already paid online)
- Certification from the Territorial Labour Directorate confirming that the professional services contract does not constitute an employment relationship. 
- Copy of the professional services contract outlining the scope and conditions of the activity. 
- Provisional nulla osta issued by the competent Questura, valid for a maximum of three months from the date of issuance. 
- Proof of suitable accommodation, such as a property deed, rental agreement, hotel reservation, or a declaration from the contracting party providing appropriate housing. 
- Proof of annual income, of lawful origin, exceeding the minimum threshold required for exemption from healthcare co‑payment. This income cannot be demonstrated through bank guarantees or insurance policies.
In the cases of self-employment with CO.CO.CO (coordinated and continuous collaborations) form, the specific additional documents are required.
- Declaration of no impediments issued by the competent Italian authority when authorizations, licenses, activity notifications, or registration in specific professional or commercial registers are required. 
- Certification of the financial resources necessary to carry out the activity, issued by the relevant Chamber of Commerce (CCIAA) or competent professional body. 
- Provisional nulla osta issued by the Questura responsible for the area where the activity will take place, valid for a maximum of three months from issuance. 
- Proof of suitable accommodation, such as a property deed, rental agreement, hotel booking, or a declaration from the contracting party confirming that suitable housing is provided. 
- Proof of annual income, of lawful origin, exceeding the minimum threshold required for exemption from healthcare co‑payment. This income cannot be demonstrated through bank guarantees or insurance policies.
For Innovative start-up founders additional documents are required.
- the nulla osta issued by the “Italia Start‑up Visa” Technical Committee (Ministry of Economic Development);
- documentation proving the availability of financial resources, of lawful origin, dedicated to the start‑up, of no less than €50,000;
- proof of suitable accommodation (hotel booking, rental contract, or declaration by a third party providing suitable housing);
- proof of having earned in the previous year in the country of origin a lawful income exceeding the minimum threshold required for exemption from healthcare co‑payment.
For renowned or highly qualified artists, additional documents are required.
- Copy of the self‑employment contract confirming remuneration higher than the amount established by national collective agreements for comparable employed workers. 
- Formal declaration from the hosting entity’s legal representative to the Territorial Labour Directorate, confirming that no employment relationship will be established. 
- Police clearance (nulla osta) issued by the competent Questura. 
- Proof of suitable accommodation, such as a hotel booking or a declaration from the contracting party confirming that appropriate housing is provided.
Documents specifications for Corporate Officers.
- Financial resource certification is not required.
- Instead, applicants must provide the company’s registration certificate from the Companies Register and a formal declaration by the legal representative to the Territorial Labour Directorate confirming that no employment relationship will be established with the applicant.
Documents specifications for Entrepreneurs.
- A business plan demonstrating the relevance of the activity.
- Proof of personal financial resources, of lawful origin, amounting to at least €500,000 for the investment project.
- A commitment to create a minimum of three new jobs in Italy.

visa_types.overview:
Overview
The National Visa – Type D allows applicants for stays longer than 90 days, regulated by Italian law.
The Self‑Employment Visa allows non‑EU nationals to enter Italy for the purpose of carrying out an independent economic activity, without an employment relationship. Eligible activities include entrepreneurial work (individual or corporate), regulated or unregulated liberal professions, and artisanal or commercial activities. Certain professional categories, such as renowned artists, athletes, and highly qualified professionals, may also apply when operating as self‑employed workers.
This visa may be issued for short‑term stays (for example, start‑up procedures, contractual arrangements, or preliminary formalities) or for long‑term stays, with fixed or open‑ended terms, intended to establish a stable professional presence in Italy.
Application for self‑employment visa is generally subject to the annual quotas established under the Decreto Flussi and applicants are strongly advised to review the relevant quota provisions before applying.
Please check the specific categories listed under the Decreto Flussi, which are permitted within the quotas for the self-employment visas:
Entrepreneurs 
Applicants intending to carry out activities of interest to the Italian economy.
Liberal Professionals
This category includes:
- Regulated or supervised professions (e.g., lawyers, doctors, engineers). The competent professional body must verify qualifications and issue the required certification. Recognition of foreign qualifications may be required under applicable sectoral rules.
- Unregulated professions, provided they are represented by national associations registered in official public administration lists and authorized to issue certificates of professional quality and qualification.
Corporate Officers
Eligible applicants are holders of specific administrative or control roles in non‑cooperative Italian companies, including: the president, a board member, the (CEO) Chief Executive Officer, the Statutory Auditor.
Innovative Start‑Up Founders
Foreign nationals intending to establish an innovative start‑up in Italy may apply under this category. Applicants must meet the requirements set by the competent Italian authorities for innovative enterprises.
Self‑employment entries outside the annual quotas are permitted for the following categories:
- Managers or highly specialized staff of foreign or Italian companies with headquarters, branches, or representative offices in Italy, provided they have been employed in the same sector for at least six months prior to their transfer.
- University language assistants participating in academic exchange programs or linguistic teaching activities.
- University professors assigned to academic positions at Italian universities.
- Professional translators and interpreters performing activities that fall within self‑employment rules.
Please note that also the following cases enter in the self-employment category, outside the annual quota:
Self‑Employed Athletes
Foreign athletes invited to Italy to perform self‑employment activities may obtain a visa upon presentation of a nominal authorization issued by CONI, accompanied by the nulla osta from the competent Questura.
Forms of work that resemble subordinate employment, such as coordinated and continuous collaborations (CO.CO.CO.), are not included within the quota categories. This applies particularly when the activity involves: a predetermined periodic remuneration, a fixed workplace, organizational constraints and long‑term continuity. 
Renowned or Highly Qualified Artists 
Short‑term self‑employment or entertainment visas are issued outside the annual quotas and may be granted on the basis of the contract alone.
The documentation must clearly confirm that the activity qualifies as self‑employment. These entries are processed outside the annual quotas.
Before submission, ensure your competency to submit your application.
Territorial competence
You must be a citizen and/or legal resident of Burkina Faso, Côte d’Ivoire, Liberia, Niger or Sierra Leone, or be legally present there. If you are only present temporarily in these Countries, then you must justify why you cannot apply where you habitually reside. 
Material competence
If the Embassy of Italy in Ivory Coast is not competent, your application and documents will be returned, visa fees refunded, and a written decision of incompetence issued. Please verify the competent Italian Embassy/Consulate before booking.

visa_types.overview:
Overview
The Uniform Schengen Visa (USV) – Type C allows applicants to travel for short stays of up to 90 days in any 180-day period.
This visa is issued to applicants (after proving the necessity of passing through the Schengen area) to allow them to pass through Italy to reach their final destination.
This visa is a short term (type “C”) period visa with a maximum of 90 days
Before submission, ensure your competency to submit your application.
Territorial competence
You must be a citizen and/or legal resident of Burkina Faso, Côte d’Ivoire, Liberia, Niger or Sierra Leone, or be legally present there. If you are only present temporarily in these Countries, then you must justify why you cannot apply where you habitually reside. 
Material competence
If the Embassy of Italy in Ivory Coast is not competent, your application and documents will be returned, visa fees refunded, and a written decision of incompetence issued. Please verify the competent Italian Embassy/Consulate before booking.

You must be a citizen and/or legal resident of Burkina Faso, Côte d’Ivoire, Liberia, Niger or Sierra Leone, or be legally present there. If you are only present temporarily in these Countries, then you must justify why you cannot apply where you habitually reside. 
Material competence
If the Embassy of Italy in Ivory Coast is not competent, your application and documents will be returned, visa fees refunded, and a written decision of incompetence issued. Please verify the competent Italian Embassy/Consulate before booking.

## Visa Fees

[ita > civ > en > Table:Transit > Visa Fees 1]
(The fee change is as per the currency exchange rates)
The above-mentioned service charges include VAT 18%. Fee amount can be paid only in local currency.
The fee will be applicable per set of documents to be legalized (each set will contain a maximum of 3 documents, (i.e) originals and translations).
Visa fees are established by the applicable legislation and are periodically updated.
No other fees apply beyond those listed on this website.

## Documents Required
Submitting all required documents does not guarantee a visa. 
Each applicant needs to complete and sign his own form and apply individually.
Checklist for Transit B Visa
- Visa application form, filled in completely in block letters, and signed by the applicant.
- Valid Passport (valid for at least 3 months longer than intended stay), +1 photocopy of the bio-data page of the said passport 
- Previous passports, (if any) +1 photocopy of pages bearing particulars of the applicant
- 2 recent passport size photographs (not more than 3 months old) 
- Visa Fee + VFS Charges (if not already paid online)
- International health insurance cover for the duration of stay
- Flight booking slip - proof of the availability of personal means of transport
- Documentation showing the reason of travelling and proving the necessity to transit through Italy
- Hotel Reservation/ Declaration of lodging from a person residing in Italy

## Photo Specifications
Photographs 
Please provide your recent photograph (not more than 6 months old). The photographs should be in color: -
- Taken against a clear background so that features are distinguishable and contrast against the background;
- Clear and of good quality;
- printed on normal photographic paper;
- Unmounted - 45 mm x 35 mm in size;
- Full face (without sunglasses and normally without a hat or other head covering, unless the applicant wears such items because of their religious belief or ethnic background).

## Processing Time
Regarding the processing times for visa applications, it is important to distinguish between Schengen visas (type C) and national visas (type D).
For Schengen visas (type C), the processing time is 15 calendar days from the date the application is deemed admissible, extendable in exceptional cases up to a maximum of 45 days if a more thorough examination of the application is required.
For national visas (type D), the general processing time is 90 days from the date of application.
Exceptions include family reunification visas and subordinate work visas, which must be decided within a maximum of 30 days, and self-employed work visas, for which the decision must be made within 120 days.
The Embassy will make efforts to expedite the decision on the application before departure date.

## Download Forms
Please find the Visa/Legalization application form below. Application form must be submitted completed and signed by the applicant. Visa application forms can be provided at the Center. 
Visa application forms are free of charge. If you have any problems downloading a form, please contact our Visa and Legalization Application Centre.

visa_types.name: Study Visa - Vocational Training
visa_types.documents_required:
Submitting all required documents does not guarantee a visa. 
Each applicant needs to complete and sign his own form and apply individually.
Checklist for Study Visa – Vocational training
- Visa application form, filled in completely in block letters, and signed by the applicant.
- For minors, the form is submitted and signed by the person exercising parental responsibility or a legal guardian. If a parent/legal guardian is not travelling with the minor, their travel consent must be provided as required by the laws of the minor’s country of residence.
- Valid Passport (valid for at least 3 months longer than intended stay), +1 photocopy of the bio-data page of the said passport 
- Previous passports, (if any) +1 photocopy of pages bearing particulars of the applicant
- 2 recent passport size photographs (not more than 3 months old) 
- Visa Fee + VFS Charges (if not already paid online)
- Documentation confirming enrolment in the vocational training course to be attended (indicating duration, programme, qualification awarded, etc.);
- Documentation relating to the education and training acquired in the country of origin;
- Dichiarazione di Valore or CIMEA Statement (where required);
- Round‑trip travel ticket (or booking);
- Proof of sufficient financial means for the requested period of stay, not lower than the parameters set out in the Directive of the Ministry of the Interior. For this purpose, bank statements and payslips, personal or of the applicant’s parents, for the previous 6 months must be submitted. If multiple bank accounts are held, the account on which the salary is paid must also be included.
- Health/medical travel insurance valid in Italy for medical care and hospitalisation, without limitations or exclusions.
- Proof of adequate accommodation (confirmed hotel reservation for at least one-month, rental contract, or declaration of hospitality).

visa_types.overview:
Overview
The Uniform Schengen Visa (USV) – Type C allows applicants to travel for short stays of up to 90 days in any 180-day period.
This visa is for foreign students who wish to attend a vocational training program in Italy aimed at obtaining a qualification or skills certification. Courses must last less than 24 months and be organized exclusively by training institutions accredited under regional regulations.
Applicants must demonstrate that the selected course is consistent with their previous education.
Special requirements apply to training in the medical and health sector:
- If the program includes clinical activities, contact with patients, or any healthcare tasks, the student must obtain prior recognition of their qualification from the Ministry of Health.
- If the program does not involve clinical activities, the hosting healthcare facility must provide a formal declaration confirming the absence of such activities.
Non‑EU doctors taking part in professional development or training programs that include clinical activities may obtain the visa based on the temporary authorization decree issued by the Ministry of Health. In this case, academic qualification recognition is not required.
Before submission, ensure your competency to submit your application.
Territorial competence
You must be a citizen and/or legal resident of Burkina Faso, Côte d’Ivoire, Liberia, Niger or Sierra Leone, or be legally present there. If you are only present temporarily in these Countries, then you must justify why you cannot apply where you habitually reside. 
Material competence
If the Embassy of Italy in Ivory Coast is not competent, your application and documents will be returned, visa fees refunded, and a written decision of incompetence issued. Please verify the competent Italian Embassy/Consulate before booking.

visa_types.name: Study Visa - University Enrollment
visa_types.documents_required:
Documentation Required for Pre‑Enrolment (Universitaly), before the visa application
Among the documents to be uploaded on Universitaly, particular importance is given to the Declaration of Value issued by the Italian Embassy in the country where the qualification was obtained, or to the CIMEA Statements of Comparability and Verification. 
The Declaration of Value certifies: - the nature of the diploma, - the duration of the educational path, - the legal value of the qualification within the local education system.
Without this document, the university cannot complete the evaluation of the application.
To obtain it, students must present:
- the original diploma,
- transcripts or relevé de notes,
- any supplementary certificates,
- an official Italian translation.
Once issued, the Declaration of Value must be uploaded to Universitaly, allowing the university to remove the conditional admission and confirm acceptance. Only after the university has lifted the reserve and approved the application may the student book an appointment and submit the visa request at the Embassy of Italy in Abidjan.
It is strongly recommended to apply between July and September, allowing sufficient time for consular processing before the start of the academic year.
Submitting all required documents does not guarantee a visa. 
Each applicant needs to complete and sign his own form and apply individually.
Checklist for Study Visa – University Enrollment
- Visa application form, filled in completely in block letters, and signed by the applicant.
- Valid Passport (valid for at least 3 months longer than intended stay), +1 photocopy of the bio-data page of the said passport 
- Previous passports, (if any) +1 photocopy of pages bearing particulars of the applicant
- 2 recent passport size photographs (not more than 3 months old) 
- Visa Fee + VFS Charges (if not already paid online)
- Language Requirements: for degree programs taught in Italian, students must demonstrate Italian language proficiency at B2 level (CEFR). Universities normally verify this through an institutional language test before the visa application, alternatively, recognized certification may be submitted.
- Pre‑admission letter issued by the university through Universitaly, confirming that “the student is admitted to the study program
- Declaration of Value or CIMEA Statement.
- Round‑trip flight ticket or booking.
- Proof of sufficient financial means, according to MUR parameters (for 2025–2026: €534.41/month; €6,947.33/year), demonstrated through personal bank statements, or those of parents/close relatives (with a formal declaration of financial support), or through scholarships.
- Health/medical insurance valid in Italy, covering medical care and hospitalization without limitations or exclusions.
- Proof of accommodation (confirmed hotel reservation for at least one month, rental contract, or declaration of hospitality).

visa_types.overview:
Overview
The National Visa – Type D allows applicants for stays longer than 90 days, regulated by Italian law.
This visa allows foreign students to travel to Italy to enroll in a degree program at an Italian University or at an Institute of Higher Education in the Arts and Music (AFAM), whether public or private. 
The first step is the pre‑enrolment on the Universitaly portal, which is the only official platform for international students applying to Italian university programs. The pre‑enrolment window generally opens between April and July each year (dates may vary based on the Ministry of Universities and Research - MUR instructions). Through the portal, students select the degree program and university, upload the required documents, and wait for the university to issue the initial clearance, necessary to further apply for the visa.
Before submission, ensure your competency to submit your application.
Territorial competence
You must be a citizen and/or legal resident of Burkina Faso, Côte d’Ivoire, Liberia, Niger or Sierra Leone, or be legally present there. If you are only present temporarily in these Countries, then you must justify why you cannot apply where you habitually reside. 
Material competence
If the Embassy of Italy in Ivory Coast is not competent, your application and documents will be returned, visa fees refunded, and a written decision of incompetence issued. Please verify the competent Italian Embassy/Consulate before booking.

visa_types.name: Family Reunification with EU Citizens visa
visa_types.documents_required:
Submitting all required documents does not guarantee a visa. 
Each applicant needs to complete and sign his own form and apply individually.
- Visa application form, filled in completely in block letters, and signed by the applicant.
For minors, the form is submitted and signed by the person exercising parental responsibility or a legal guardian. If a parent/legal guardian is not travelling with the minor, their travel consent must be provided as required by the laws of the minor’s country of residence.
- Valid Passport (valid for at least 3 months longer than intended stay), +1 photocopy of the bio-data page of the said passport 
- Previous passports, (if any) +1 photocopy of pages bearing particulars of the applicant
- 2 recent passport size photographs (not more than 3 months old) 
- Visa Fee + VFS Charges (if not already paid online)
- Proof of the family or marital relationship with the Italian or EU citizen. Examples include:
- For spouse / partner in a same‑sex civil union: marriage certificate and certificate of non‑divorce and non‑separation.
- For minor children: full birth certificate, adoption decree, or judicial order of foster care/guardianship, together with the travel consent of the non‑accompanying parent.
- For children over 21: proof of dependency, demonstrated through bank transfer receipts for the two years preceding the visa application and the current year, complete and organised by month.
- For dependent parents: full birth certificates of both the foreign national legally residing in Italy and of the applicant and proof of dependency on the Italian/EU citizen, demonstrated through bank transfer receipts for the two years preceding the visa application and the current year, complete and organised by month.
Important Notes
Legalisation and Translation

visa_types.overview:
Overview
The Uniform Schengen Visa (USV) – Type C allows applicants to travel for short stays of up to 90 days in any 180-day period.
This visa applies to family members of Italian citizens and of “active” EU citizens, meaning EU nationals who exercise their right to free movement. In these cases, the visa is issued free of charge, processed with priority, and no Nulla Osta is required.
The eligibly family members who can apply for a Family reunification with EU Citizens include:
- Spouse or partner in a same‑sex civil union;
- Children under 21, or children over 21 who are dependent, including those of the spouse or civil union partner;
- Dependent parents, including those of the spouse or civil union partner.
Other special cases who could request a family reunion with EU Citizen visa are:
- Additional family members do not have an automatic right to a family visa, but the Member State must facilitate their entry when they can demonstrate that they are cohabiting with or economically dependent on the EU citizen in their country of origin.
- The same provision applies to a partner in a stable, duly attested relationship, even if not formalised as a marriage or civil union. In such cases, the Embassy will assess the documentation provided, such as proof of cohabitation, financial support, or consistent correspondence, to verify the genuine and durable nature of the relationship.
Before submission, ensure your competency to submit your application.
Territorial competence
You must be a citizen and/or legal resident of Burkina Faso, Côte d’Ivoire, Liberia, Niger or Sierra Leone, or be legally present there. If you are only present temporarily in these Countries, then you must justify why you cannot apply where you habitually reside. 
Material competence
If the Embassy of Italy in Ivory Coast is not competent, your application and documents will be returned, visa fees refunded, and a written decision of incompetence issued. Please verify the competent Italian Embassy/Consulate before booking.

(The fee change is as per the currency exchange rates)
The above-mentioned service charges include VAT 18%. Fee amount can be paid only in local currency.
The fee will be applicable per set of documents to be legalized (each set will contain a maximum of 3 documents, (i.e) originals and translations).
Visa fees are established by the applicable legislation and are periodically updated.
No other fees apply beyond those listed on this website.

## Documents Required
Submitting all required documents does not guarantee a visa. 
Each applicant needs to complete and sign his own form and apply individually.
- Visa application form, filled in completely in block letters, and signed by the applicant.
For minors, the form is submitted and signed by the person exercising parental responsibility or a legal guardian. If a parent/legal guardian is not travelling with the minor, their travel consent must be provided as required by the laws of the minor’s country of residence.
- Valid Passport (valid for at least 3 months longer than intended stay), +1 photocopy of the bio-data page of the said passport 
- Previous passports, (if any) +1 photocopy of pages bearing particulars of the applicant
- 2 recent passport size photographs (not more than 3 months old) 
- Visa Fee + VFS Charges (if not already paid online)
- Proof of the family or marital relationship with the Italian or EU citizen. Examples include:
- For spouse / partner in a same‑sex civil union: marriage certificate and certificate of non‑divorce and non‑separation.
- For minor children: full birth certificate, adoption decree, or judicial order of foster care/guardianship, together with the travel consent of the non‑accompanying parent.
- For children over 21: proof of dependency, demonstrated through bank transfer receipts for the two years preceding the visa application and the current year, complete and organised by month.
- For dependent parents: full birth certificates of both the foreign national legally residing in Italy and of the applicant and proof of dependency on the Italian/EU citizen, demonstrated through bank transfer receipts for the two years preceding the visa application and the current year, complete and organised by month.

Legalisation and Translation

The submission of false or forged documents is a criminal offence. It results in visa refusal and the transmission of a criminal report to the competent Italian Public Prosecutor’s Office.

## Photo Specifications
Photographs 
Please provide your recent photograph (not more than 6 months old). The photographs should be in color: - 
- Taken against a clear background so that features are distinguishable and contrast against the background;
- Clear and of good quality;
- printed on normal photographic paper;
- Unmounted - 45 mm x 35 mm in size;
- Full face (without sunglasses and normally without a hat or other head covering, unless the applicant wears such items because of their religious belief or ethnic background).

## Processing Time
Regarding the processing times for visa applications, it is important to distinguish between Schengen visas (type C) and national visas (type D).
For Schengen visas (type C), the processing time is 15 calendar days from the date the application is deemed admissible, extendable in exceptional cases up to a maximum of 45 days if a more thorough examination of the application is required.

visa_types.documents_required:
Submitting all required documents does not guarantee a visa. 
Each applicant needs to complete and sign his own form and apply individually.
Checklist for Adoption Visa
- Visa application form, filled in completely in block letters, and signed by the applicant.
- For minors, the form is submitted and signed by the person exercising parental responsibility or a legal guardian. If a parent/legal guardian is not travelling with the minor, their travel consent must be provided as required by the laws of the minor’s country of residence.
- Valid Passport (valid for at least 3 months longer than intended stay), +1 photocopy of the bio-data page of the said passport 
- Previous passports, (if any) +1 photocopy of pages bearing particulars of the applicant
- 2 recent passport size photographs (not more than 3 months old) 
- Visa Fee + VFS Charges (if not already paid online)
- Nominal authorization for entry and permanent stay in Italy issued by the Commission for International Adoptions (CAI), in the cases provided under Articles 32 and 39(h) of Law 184/1983; or
- CAI issued Nulla Osta for all other situations, including when a foreign adoption order has already been recognized in Italy,
At the time of submitting the visa application, both adoptive parents must be present. If one parent is temporarily absent, a signed power of attorney must be provided.
Special rules apply to international adoptions: an adoption pronounced abroad upon request of Italian citizens who can prove at least two years of continuous residence in that country at the time of the ruling is recognized in Italy by the Juvenile Court, provided it complies with the principles of the Convention. However, for the issuance of the adoption visa, the CAI Nulla Osta is always required, even when the foreign ruling has already been recognized.

visa_types.overview:
Overview
The National Visa – Type D allows applicants for stays longer than 90 days, regulated by Italian law. 
The medical treatment visa allows foreign nationals to enter Italy for a short or long‑term stay, for a limited period, in order to undergo medical treatment in accredited public or private healthcare facilities. 
The visa application may be submitted by the applicant himself, a family member, or another person with a legitimate interest. A visa may also be issued to an accompanying person, provided they have sufficient financial means for the duration of the stay.
For long stays, the residence permit issued for medical treatment is valid for the duration of the therapeutic course, may be renewed as long as clinical needs are documented, and allows the holder to work.
For basic medical check‑ups, a tourist visa may be issued if all legal conditions are met. The Embassy may request a medical certificate, issued by a doctor appointed by the Embassy, confirming that the required treatment is not available in the applicant’s country of residence.
Before submission, ensure your competency to submit your application.
Territorial competence
You must be a citizen and/or legal resident of Burkina Faso, Côte d’Ivoire, Liberia, Niger or Sierra Leone, or be legally present there. If you are only present temporarily in these Countries, then you must justify why you cannot apply where you habitually reside. 
Material competence
If the Embassy of Italy in Ivory Coast is not competent, your application and documents will be returned, visa fees refunded, and a written decision of incompetence issued. Please verify the competent Italian Embassy/Consulate before booking.

visa_types.overview:
Overview
The National Visa – Type D allows applicants for stays longer than 90 days, regulated by Italian law.
The Mission Visa allows foreign nationals to enter Italy, either for short or long stays, for activities connected to their public functions or assignments of public utility. It is primarily intended for:
- Government officials
- Public administration employees
- Representatives of state bodies
- Staff of international organizations who are required to travel to Italy to carry out their institutional duties.
The visa may also be issued to private individuals when the purpose of the trip or the activity to be carried out is considered of public interest for relations between their country and Italy.
Co‑habiting immediate family members travelling with the principal applicant may be granted a corresponding mission visa.
Other accompanying persons who are not part of the immediate family unit may apply for a tourist visa, provided they meet the applicable requirements.
The mission visa may also be issued to foreign diplomatic officials undertaking short official stays that do not require formal accreditation or notification. In these cases, authorization from the Diplomatic Protocol of the Italian Republic is required, and the applicant must obtain a diplomatic visa.
Important Note - Requests for mission visas for personnel of: FAO, IFAD, WFP, Bioversity International and IDLO must be submitted through the Permanent Representation of Italy to the United Nations Agencies in Rome.
Before submission, ensure your competency to submit your application.
Territorial competence
You must be a citizen and/or legal resident of Burkina Faso, Côte d’Ivoire, Liberia, Niger or Sierra Leone, or be legally present there. If you are only present temporarily in these Countries, then you must justify why you cannot apply where you habitually reside. 
Material competence
If the Embassy of Italy in Ivory Coast is not competent, your application and documents will be returned, visa fees refunded, and a written decision of incompetence issued. Please verify the competent Italian Embassy/Consulate before booking.

visa_types.overview:
Overview
The Uniform Schengen Visa (USV) – Type C allows applicants to travel for short stays of up to 90 days in any 180-day period.
The sports competition visa allows athletes and accompanying technical staff - such as coaches, technical directors, and trainers-to travel to Italy for short‑term participation in professional or amateur sporting events organized by National Sports Federations or Associated Sports Disciplines recognized by CONI. The visa is issued only for official or friendly events within disciplines recognized by the Italian National Olympic Committee.
Participation must be confirmed through a CONI Communication, which certifies the importance of the event, confirms the invitation to the athlete or the team, and formally requests the issuance of the visa. For teams or sports groups, references will be made to official lists provided by recognized foreign sports federations, including the names and roles of each participant.
If the CONI Communication is not provided, a tourist visa may be issued instead, provided that an invitation is submitted and all requirements for that category are met.
For minor applicants, a travel authorization is required. It must be signed by each non‑accompanying parent holding parental authority, or by the legal guardian, and must comply with the regulations in the minor’s country of residence.
Before submission, ensure your competency to submit your application.
Territorial competence
You must be a citizen and/or legal resident of Burkina Faso, Côte d’Ivoire, Liberia, Niger or Sierra Leone, or be legally present there. If you are only present temporarily in these Countries, then you must justify why you cannot apply where you habitually reside. 
Material competence
If the Embassy of Italy in Ivory Coast is not competent, your application and documents will be returned, visa fees refunded, and a written decision of incompetence issued. Please verify the competent Italian Embassy/Consulate before booking.

Checklist for Medical Treatment
- Visa application form, filled in completely in block letters, and signed by the applicant.

- Round‑trip travel ticket or booking also for any accompanying person.

Overview
The Uniform Schengen Visa (USV) – Type C allows applicants to travel for short stays of up to 90 days in any 180-day period.
The medical treatment visa allows foreign nationals to enter Italy for a short or long‑term stay, for a limited period, in order to undergo medical treatment in accredited public or private healthcare facilities. 
The visa application may be submitted by the applicant himself, a family member, or another person with a legitimate interest. A visa may also be issued to an accompanying person, provided they have sufficient financial means for the duration of the stay.
For long stays, the residence permit issued for medical treatment is valid for the duration of the therapeutic course, may be renewed as long as clinical needs are documented, and allows the holder to work.
For basic medical check‑ups, a tourist visa may be issued if all legal conditions are met. The Embassy may request a medical certificate, issued by a doctor appointed by the Embassy, confirming that the required treatment is not available in the applicant’s country of residence.
Before submission, ensure your competency to submit your application.
Territorial competence
You must be a citizen and/or legal resident of Burkina Faso, Côte d’Ivoire, Liberia, Niger or Sierra Leone, or be legally present there. If you are only present temporarily in these Countries, then you must justify why you cannot apply where you habitually reside. 
Material competence
If the Embassy of Italy in Ivory Coast is not competent, your application and documents will be returned, visa fees refunded, and a written decision of incompetence issued. Please verify the competent Italian Embassy/Consulate before booking.

Overview
The National Visa – Type D allows applicants for stays longer than 90 days, regulated by Italian law.
This visa is intended for foreign religious personnel and ministers of worship, belonging to recognized religious organizations who wish to enter Italy for short or long term stays to participate in religious events or to carry out ecclesiastical, religious, or pastoral activities.
Individuals who are not religious personnel but intend to travel for pilgrimages or other religious events may apply for a tourist visa, provided they meet the relevant eligibility criteria.
Before submission, ensure your competency to submit your application.
Territorial competence
You must be a citizen and/or legal resident of Burkina Faso, Côte d’Ivoire, Liberia, Niger or Sierra Leone, or be legally present there. If you are only present temporarily in these Countries, then you must justify why you cannot apply where you habitually reside. 
Material competence
If the Embassy of Italy in Ivory Coast is not competent, your application and documents will be returned, visa fees refunded, and a written decision of incompetence issued. Please verify the competent Italian Embassy/Consulate before booking.

visa_types.documents_required:
Submitting all required documents does not guarantee a visa. 
Each applicant needs to complete and sign his own form and apply individually.
- General documents required Visa application form, filled in completely in block letters, and signed by the applicant.
- For minors, the form is submitted and signed by the person exercising parental responsibility or a legal guardian. If a parent/legal guardian is not travelling with the minor, their travel consent must be provided as required by the laws of the minor’s country of residence.
- Valid Passport (valid for at least 3 months longer than intended stay), +1 photocopy of the bio-data page of the said passport 
- Previous passports, (if any) +1 photocopy of pages bearing particulars of the applicant
- 2 recent passport size photographs (not more than 3 months old) 
- Visa Fee + VFS Charges (if not already paid online)
- Certification from the Territorial Labour Directorate confirming that the professional services contract does not constitute an employment relationship. 
- Copy of the professional services contract outlining the scope and conditions of the activity. 
- Provisional nulla osta issued by the competent Questura, valid for a maximum of three months from the date of issuance. 
- Proof of suitable accommodation, such as a property deed, rental agreement, hotel reservation, or a declaration from the contracting party providing appropriate housing. 
- Proof of annual income, of lawful origin, exceeding the minimum threshold required for exemption from healthcare co‑payment. This income cannot be demonstrated through bank guarantees or insurance policies.
In the cases of self-employment with CO.CO.CO (coordinated and continuous collaborations) form, the specific additional documents are required.
- Declaration of no impediments issued by the competent Italian authority when authorizations, licenses, activity notifications, or registration in specific professional or commercial registers are required. 
- Certification of the financial resources necessary to carry out the activity, issued by the relevant Chamber of Commerce (CCIAA) or competent professional body. 
- Provisional nulla osta issued by the Questura responsible for the area where the activity will take place, valid for a maximum of three months from issuance. 
- Proof of suitable accommodation, such as a property deed, rental agreement, hotel booking, or a declaration from the contracting party confirming that suitable housing is provided. 
- Proof of annual income, of lawful origin, exceeding the minimum threshold required for exemption from healthcare co‑payment. This income cannot be demonstrated through bank guarantees or insurance policies.
For Innovative start-up founders additional documents are required.
- the nulla osta issued by the “Italia Start‑up Visa” Technical Committee (Ministry of Economic Development);
- documentation proving the availability of financial resources, of lawful origin, dedicated to the start‑up, of no less than €50,000;
- proof of suitable accommodation (hotel booking, rental contract, or declaration by a third party providing suitable housing);
- proof of having earned in the previous year in the country of origin a lawful income exceeding the minimum threshold required for exemption from healthcare co‑payment.
For renowned or highly qualified artists, additional documents are required.
- Copy of the self‑employment contract confirming remuneration higher than the amount established by national collective agreements for comparable employed workers. 
- Formal declaration from the hosting entity’s legal representative to the Territorial Labour Directorate, confirming that no employment relationship will be established. 
- Police clearance (nulla osta) issued by the competent Questura. 
- Proof of suitable accommodation, such as a hotel booking or a declaration from the contracting party confirming that appropriate housing is provided.
Documents specifications for Corporate Officers.
- Financial resource certification is not required.
- Instead, applicants must provide the company’s registration certificate from the Companies Register and a formal declaration by the legal representative to the Territorial Labour Directorate confirming that no employment relationship will be established with the applicant.
Documents specifications for Entrepreneurs. 
- A business plan demonstrating the relevance of the activity.
- Proof of personal financial resources, of lawful origin, amounting to at least €500,000 for the investment project.
- A commitment to create a minimum of three new jobs in Italy.

visa_types.overview:
Overview
The Uniform Schengen Visa (USV) – Type C allows applicants to travel for short stays of up to 90 days in any 180-day period.
The Self‑Employment Visa allows non‑EU nationals to enter Italy for the purpose of carrying out an independent economic activity, without an employment relationship. Eligible activities include entrepreneurial work (individual or corporate), regulated or unregulated liberal professions, and artisanal or commercial activities. Certain professional categories, such as renowned artists, athletes, and highly qualified professionals, may also apply when operating as self‑employed workers.
This visa may be issued for short‑term stays (for example, start‑up procedures, contractual arrangements, or preliminary formalities) or for long‑term stays, with fixed or open‑ended terms, intended to establish a stable professional presence in Italy.
Application for self‑employment visa is generally subject to the annual quotas established under the Decreto Flussi and applicants are strongly advised to review the relevant quota provisions before applying.
Please check the specific categories listed under the Decreto Flussi, which are permitted within the quotas for the self-employment visas:
Entrepreneurs
Applicants intending to carry out activities of interest to the Italian economy.
Liberal Professionals
This category includes:
- Regulated or supervised professions (e.g., lawyers, doctors, engineers). The competent professional body must verify qualifications and issue the required certification. Recognition of foreign qualifications may be required under applicable sectoral rules.
- Unregulated professions, provided they are represented by national associations registered in official public administration lists and authorized to issue certificates of professional quality and qualification.
Corporate Officers
Eligible applicants are holders of specific administrative or control roles in non‑cooperative Italian companies, including: the president, a board member, the (CEO) Chief Executive Officer, the Statutory Auditor.
Innovative Start‑Up Founders
Foreign nationals intending to establish an innovative start‑up in Italy may apply under this category. Applicants must meet the requirements set by the competent Italian authorities for innovative enterprises.
Self‑employment entries outside the annual quotas are permitted for the following categories:
- Managers or highly specialized staff of foreign or Italian companies with headquarters, branches, or representative offices in Italy, provided they have been employed in the same sector for at least six months prior to their transfer.
- University language assistants participating in academic exchange programs or linguistic teaching activities.
- University professors assigned to academic positions at Italian universities.
- Professional translators and interpreters performing activities that fall within self‑employment rules.
Please note that also the following cases enter in the self-employment category, outside the annual quota:
Self‑Employed Athletes 
Foreign athletes invited to Italy to perform self‑employment activities may obtain a visa upon presentation of a nominal authorization issued by CONI, accompanied by the nulla osta from the competent Questura.
Forms of work that resemble subordinate employment, such as coordinated and continuous collaborations (CO.CO.CO.), are not included within the quota categories. This applies particularly when the activity involves: a predetermined periodic remuneration, a fixed workplace, organizational constraints and long‑term continuity. 
Renowned or Highly Qualified Artists
Short‑term self‑employment or entertainment visas are issued outside the annual quotas and may be granted on the basis of the contract alone.
The documentation must clearly confirm that the activity qualifies as self‑employment. These entries are processed outside the annual quotas.
Before submission, ensure your competency to submit your application.
Territorial competence
You must be a citizen and/or legal resident of Burkina Faso, Côte d’Ivoire, Liberia, Niger or Sierra Leone, or be legally present there. If you are only present temporarily in these Countries, then you must justify why you cannot apply where you habitually reside. 
Material competence
If the Embassy of Italy in Ivory Coast is not competent, your application and documents will be returned, visa fees refunded, and a written decision of incompetence issued. Please verify the competent Italian Embassy/Consulate before booking.

visa_types.name: National visas (Type D)
visa_types.overview:
Overview
Type D visas allow stays over 90 days and allow entry into Italy to obtain a residence permit. The visa validity is the window to enter Italy; the permit will define the permitted stay.
The main national visas categories are study (school/university), employment or self‑employment, family reunification, mission, volunteering, research, elective residence, long‑term medical treatment, etc..
Holders of a National visa may travel in Schengen for up to 90 days in any 180 days, but long‑term residence is permitted only in Italy.
The general rule related to the processing time for a National Visa is:
- 90 days from date of application.
- 30 days for family reunification and subordinate work applications.
- 120 days for self‑employment applications
If, however, the processing is suspended for document checks and verification of the documentation submitted, you will receive written notice (to be signed for acknowledgment) and the processing time might be extended.
It is important that applicants provide a valid and personal mobile phone and email address to ensure timely communication with VFS or the Embassy during the visa process.
The Embassy may, at any stage of the procedure, request the applicant for a consular interview or request additional documentation beyond what has been submitted by the applicant.
Biometric data (fingerprints) are required for all visa applications. As of January 2025, fingerprinting is also mandatory for national visas (type D).
For visa refusals related to family reunification, the appeal must be submitted by the family member residing in Italy before the competent Italian Ordinary Court based on their place of residence. No statutory deadline applies, and the appeal may be filed at any time after the refusal.

visa_types.name: Mission Visa short stay
visa_types.documents_required:
Submitting all required documents does not guarantee a visa. 
Each applicant needs to complete and sign his own form and apply individually.
Checklist for Mission Visa
- Visa application form, filled in completely in block letters, and signed by the applicant.
- For minors, the form is submitted and signed by the person exercising parental responsibility or a legal guardian. If a parent/legal guardian is not travelling with the minor, their travel consent must be provided as required by the laws of the minor’s country of residence.
- Valid Passport (valid for at least 3 months longer than intended stay), +1 photocopy of the bio-data page of the said passport 
- Previous passports, (if any) +1 photocopy of pages bearing particulars of the applicant
- 2 recent passport size photographs (not more than 3 months old) 
- Visa Fee + VFS Charges (if not already paid online)
- Official documentation confirming the applicant’s status as a public official or representative of an international organization assigned to Italy to perform institutional duties, for example: a Note Verbale, a Mission Order, or official communication from the competent authority confirming the institutional nature of the assignment. 
- Round‑trip travel ticket or booking. 
- Health insurance valid throughout the Schengen Area, with a minimum coverage of €30,000 for medical expenses and repatriation
- Proof of accommodation, such as a hotel reservation or a declaration from the hosting entity confirming that suitable lodging will be provided.

For Schengen visas (type C), the processing time is 15 calendar days from the date the application is deemed admissible, extendable in exceptional cases up to a maximum of 45 days if a more thorough examination of the application is required.

visa_types.overview:
Overview
The Uniform Schengen Visa (USV) – Type C allows applicants to travel for short stays of up to 90 days in any 180-day period.
The Mission Visa allows foreign nationals to enter Italy, either for short or long stays, for activities connected to their public functions or assignments of public utility. It is primarily intended for:
- Government officials
- Public administration employees
- Representatives of state bodies
- Staff of international organizations who are required to travel to Italy to carry out their institutional duties.
The visa may also be issued to private individuals when the purpose of the trip or the activity to be carried out is considered of public interest for relations between their country and Italy.
Co‑habiting immediate family members travelling with the principal applicant may be granted a corresponding mission visa.
Other accompanying persons who are not part of the immediate family unit may apply for a tourist visa, provided they meet the applicable requirements.
The mission visa may also be issued to foreign diplomatic officials undertaking short official stays that do not require formal accreditation or notification. In these cases, authorization from the Diplomatic Protocol of the Italian Republic is required, and the applicant must obtain a diplomatic visa.
Important Note - Requests for mission visas for personnel of: FAO, IFAD, WFP, Bioversity International and IDLO must be submitted through the Permanent Representation of Italy to the United Nations Agencies in Rome.
Before submission, ensure your competency to submit your application.
Territorial competence
You must be a citizen and/or legal resident of Burkina Faso, Côte d’Ivoire, Liberia, Niger or Sierra Leone, or be legally present there. If you are only present temporarily in these Countries, then you must justify why you cannot apply where you habitually reside. 
Material competence
If the Embassy of Italy in Ivory Coast is not competent, your application and documents will be returned, visa fees refunded, and a written decision of incompetence issued. Please verify the competent Italian Embassy/Consulate before booking.

visa_types.name: Subordinate Employment Visa
visa_types.documents_required:
Submitting all required documents does not guarantee a visa. 
Each applicant needs to complete and sign his own form and apply individually.
Checklist for Subordinate Employment Visa
- Visa application form, filled in completely in block letters, and signed by the applicant.
- For minors, the form is submitted and signed by the person exercising parental responsibility or a legal guardian. If a parent/legal guardian is not travelling with the minor, their travel consent must be provided as required by the laws of the minor’s country of residence.
- Valid Passport (valid for at least 3 months longer than intended stay), +1 photocopy of the bio-data page of the said passport 
- Previous passports, (if any) +1 photocopy of pages bearing particulars of the applicant
- 2 recent passport size photographs (not more than 3 months old) 
- Visa Fee + VFS Charges (if not already paid online)
- Work authorization (nulla osta) issued by the competent Single Immigration Desk (SUI). 
- Curriculum vitae, accompanied by supporting documents such as academic and/or professional qualifications and reference letters from previous employers. 
- Updated company registration extract (visura camerale) and financial documentation, including balance sheets, tax returns, or other evidence demonstrating economic solidity and actual business activity (e.g., company website, photos of premises, licences, authorizations). 
- For domestic employers: proof of the household’s financial capacity (e.g., previous year’s income tax return).
- Copy of the employment contract or job offer submitted to the SUI, detailing duties, working hours, workplace, and salary, to ensure compliance with national collective labour agreements (CCNL). 
- Information on the applicant’s family and housing situation in the country of origin, used to assess migration risk. 
- Proof of accommodation in Italy, if not already provided by the employer. 
- For domestic and home‑care sector visas: birth certificates of the applicant (and spouse, if applicable), their parents, and the employer and their parents.

visa_types.overview:
Overview
The National Visa – Type D allows applicants for stays longer than 90 days, regulated by Italian law
The Employment Visa allows foreign nationals to work in Italy under a subordinate employment relationship with an Italian employer or a foreign employer legally operating within the country.
Entry for subordinate work is generally subject to the annual quotas established under the Decreto Flussi Applicants are strongly encouraged to consult the relevant quota provisions before applying.
Except for specific exempt categories, issuing an employment visa requires prior obtainment of a work authorization (nulla osta) from the Single Immigration Desk (Sportello Unico per l’Immigrazione – SUI).
The nulla osta is issued after verification and once issued, it is transmitted electronically by the SUI to the competent consular office and must be used within:
- 6 months for hires under the Decreto Flussi
- 4 months for special cases (entries outside quotas or subject to special procedures)
After these periods, the nulla osta expires and must be requested again by the employer.
After receiving the visa and entering Italy, the worker must report to the SUI for contract signature, confirming the employer’s commitments and apply for the residence permit for subordinate work at the competent Questura.
Important Note: for employment in the domestic or home‑care sector (housekeepers, caregivers, home assistants), the Decreto Flussi prohibits hiring relatives of the assisted person or employer up to the second degree, including:parents, grandparents,siblings, grandchildren, parents‑in‑law, sons/daughters‑in‑lawandbrothers/sisters‑in‑law.
Below is the list of special cases for subordinate employment visa and cases who do not require a subordinate work visa:
Healthcare Professions
Issuing a visa for healthcare professions requires:
- Prior recognition of the professional qualification by the Italian Ministry of Health.
- If the role within a healthcare facility does not involve regulated healthcare tasks, the application must include a declaration from the facility’s legal representative confirming that no professional recognition is required.
Foreign Athletes
Employment visas for foreign athletes, professional or amateur, are issued based on a nominal authorization from CONI.
For minor athletes, both parental travel authorization and an additional authorization from the Territorial Labour Directorate, confirming compliance with child protection regulations.
Foreign Journalists (Correspondents)
Journalists assigned to Italy as official correspondents:
- Do not require a SUI nulla osta.
- Must obtain authorization from the Press Service of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs (MAECI) before the visa can be issued.
Application steps:
- Register in the “Journalists’ Area” on the MAECI website.
- Complete the online form and upload a letter from the Editor‑in‑Chief confirming the appointment, role, and duration.
- Receive a unique identification number, which must be communicated to the journalist’s home‑country diplomatic mission in Italy.
- The mission then submits a Note Verbale requesting accreditation.
Family members may apply for a mission visa, following the same authorization and accreditation procedures.
Media Crews (TV, Radio, Film, Press)
Media crew members travelling to Italy for short professional assignments must apply for a Business Visa, provided they meet the requirements for that visa category.
Seafarers
Seafarers on Italian‑flagged vessels must obtain a Maritime Employment Visa based on a formal request from the shipping company, an individual employment contract, a service contract for onboard activities and the vessel’s International Register certificate
If the individual is only joining or leaving the vessel without performing work in Italy, a Transit Visa is sufficient.
Workers Assigned to Vatican Entities
Foreign nationals called to work for departments or bodies of the Vatican City State fall outside Italian employment categories.
They must apply for an Elective Residence Visa, issued on the basis of a certificate from the Vatican entity, authenticated by the Secretariat of State, confirming: the work assignment, the accommodation and the inclusion of the worker (and accompanying family members) in the Vatican healthcare system
Before submission, ensure your competency to submit your application.
Territorial competence
You must be a citizen and/or legal resident of Burkina Faso, Côte d’Ivoire, Liberia, Niger or Sierra Leone, or be legally present there. If you are only present temporarily in these Countries, then you must justify why you cannot apply where you habitually reside. 
Material competence
If the Embassy of Italy in Ivory Coast is not competent, your application and documents will be returned, visa fees refunded, and a written decision of incompetence issued. Please verify the competent Italian Embassy/Consulate before booking.

After these periods, the nulla osta expires and must be requested again by the employer.
After receiving the visa and entering Italy, the worker must report to the SUI for contract signature, confirming the employer’s commitments and apply for the residence permit for subordinate work at the competent Questura.
Important Note: for employment in the domestic or home‑care sector (housekeepers, caregivers, home assistants), the Decreto Flussi prohibits hiring relatives of the assisted person or employer up to the second degree, including:parents, grandparents,siblings, grandchildren, parents‑in‑law, sons/daughters‑in‑lawandbrothers/sisters‑in‑law.
Below is the list of special cases for subordinate employment visa and cases who do not require a subordinate work visa:
Healthcare Professions
Issuing a visa for healthcare professions requires:
- Prior recognition of the professional qualification by the Italian Ministry of Health.
- If the role within a healthcare facility does not involve regulated healthcare tasks, the application must include a declaration from the facility’s legal representative confirming that no professional recognition is required.
Foreign Athletes
Employment visas for foreign athletes, professional or amateur, are issued based on a nominal authorization from CONI.
For minor athletes, both parental travel authorization and an additional authorization from the Territorial Labour Directorate, confirming compliance with child protection regulations.
Foreign Journalists (Correspondents)
Journalists assigned to Italy as official correspondents:
- Do not require a SUI nulla osta.
- Must obtain authorization from the Press Service of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs (MAECI) before the visa can be issued.
Application steps:
- Register in the “Journalists’ Area” on the MAECI website.
- Complete the online form and upload a letter from the Editor‑in‑Chief confirming the appointment, role, and duration.
- Receive a unique identification number, which must be communicated to the journalist’s home‑country diplomatic mission in Italy.
- The mission then submits a Note Verbale requesting accreditation.
Family members may apply for a mission visa, following the same authorization and accreditation procedures.
Media Crews (TV, Radio, Film, Press)
Media crew members travelling to Italy for short professional assignments must apply for a Business Visa, provided they meet the requirements for that visa category.
Seafarers
Seafarers on Italian‑flagged vessels must obtain a Maritime Employment Visa based on a formal request from the shipping company, an individual employment contract, a service contract for onboard activities and the vessel’s International Register certificate
If the individual is only joining or leaving the vessel without performing work in Italy, a Transit Visa is sufficient.
Workers Assigned to Vatican Entities
Foreign nationals called to work for departments or bodies of the Vatican City State fall outside Italian employment categories.
They must apply for an Elective Residence Visa, issued on the basis of a certificate from the Vatican entity, authenticated by the Secretariat of State, confirming: the work assignment, the accommodation and the inclusion of the worker (and accompanying family members) in the Vatican healthcare system
Before submission, ensure your competency to submit your application.
Territorial competence
You must be a citizen and/or legal resident of Burkina Faso, Côte d’Ivoire, Liberia, Niger or Sierra Leone, or be legally present there. If you are only present temporarily in these Countries, then you must justify why you cannot apply where you habitually reside. 
Material competence
If the Embassy of Italy in Ivory Coast is not competent, your application and documents will be returned, visa fees refunded, and a written decision of incompetence issued. Please verify the competent Italian Embassy/Consulate before booking.
